title: Kundalini and Telepathy: When You Start Knowing Things You Shouldn't Know
meta_description: Hey. You're probably reading this because you're scared. You feel like you're losing your mind, right? Like there's this internal earthquake happening, and
body:
Kundalini and Telepathy: When You Start Knowing Things You Shouldn't Know

Hey. You're probably reading this because you're scared. You feel like you're losing your mind, right? Like there's this internal earthquake happening, and suddenly you're picking up on things, knowing stuff you have no business knowing. I get it. I've been there. That 3 AM panic, the feeling of being utterly alone with what feels like a secret insanity - I know it well. This isn't some woo-woo fantasy; it's real, and it's happening to you. You're not crazy. You're just experiencing something intense, something most people don't even believe exists. And one of those things is this weird, unsettling ability to just… know. Telepathy, they call it. When Kundalini wakes up, it doesn't just rearrange your insides; it rewires your perception of reality, and that includes other people's thoughts and feelings.

I'll tell you what, when it first hit me, I thought I was hallucinating. I'd be in a conversation, and before the person even opened their mouth, I'd know what they were going to say. Or I'd get a sudden, sharp feeling about someone, completely out of the blue, and later find out it was spot on. It's disorienting, to say the least. It makes you question everything you thought you knew about your own mind, and about the world. You're suddenly an open channel, and it feels like there's no off switch. That's the terrifying part, isn't it? The lack of control. The feeling that your boundaries are dissolving, and you're merging with everything and everyone around you.

Look, I'm not here to sugarcoat it. This isn't always a pleasant experience. It can be overwhelming, confusing, and frankly, pretty damn isolating. How do you explain to your friends or family that you just knew their secret, or that you felt their pain before they even expressed it? You don't, not usually. Because they won't get it. They'll probably think you're having a psychotic break. And honestly, for a while, you might even think you are too. But you're not. This is part of the process, a side effect of a deeper energetic shift happening within you. It's intense, but it's not madness. It's an expansion of consciousness, and it's a hell of a ride.

The Energetic Shift: Why You're Suddenly "Psychic"

So, why is this happening? You've got this immense energy, Kundalini, stirring within you. Think of it like an internal power plant that's just come online, but it's got no clear wiring diagram yet. It's blasting open your energetic centers, your chakras, and it's connecting them in ways they haven't been connected before. This isn't just about feeling energy in your spine; it's about your entire nervous system getting an upgrade. Your senses become hypersensitive. Your intuition goes through the roof. And yes, your ability to pick up on other people's thoughts and emotions - that "telepathy" - becomes incredibly strong.

It's not magic, not really. It's more like your brain is suddenly able to tune into frequencies it couldn't before. We're all interconnected on some level, right? We've all had those moments where we think of someone and they call, or we finish someone's sentence. But with Kundalini active, that connection gets amplified a thousandfold. It's like your personal antenna just got a massive boost. You're not just picking up on subtle cues anymore; you're getting direct downloads. It can feel like their thoughts are your thoughts, their emotions are your emotions. It's a blurring of lines, and it can be incredibly disorienting. You might find yourself questioning, "Is this mine? Or is this theirs?" That's a common experience, and it's something you'll learn to discern over time, with practice and a lot of self-awareness.

I remember one time, I was sitting across from a friend, and I suddenly felt this overwhelming wave of sadness, deep, gut-wrenching grief. I hadn't said anything, they hadn't said anything, but I just knew they were carrying something heavy. Later, they broke down and told me about a loss they'd been hiding. It was validating, sure, but also incredibly unsettling. It made me realize how permeable my own boundaries had become. This isn't just about knowing what's for dinner; it's about tapping into the raw, unfiltered emotional and mental states of others. It's a huge responsibility, and it can feel like a burden when you're not prepared for it.

The Overload: When Knowing Too Much Becomes a Problem

Here's the thing: knowing too much, too often, without any control, is exhausting. It's like trying to listen to a hundred radio stations at once. You can't focus on your own thoughts, your own feelings, because you're constantly bombarded by everyone else's. Your nervous system is already on overdrive with the Kundalini energy, and adding this constant influx of external data just makes it worse. You'll find yourself drained, irritable, and craving solitude. You'll want to retreat, to hide away from people, just to get some peace and quiet in your own head.

I've been there. I've had to leave social situations abruptly because the sheer volume of unfiltered thoughts and emotions from others was just too much. It felt like I was drowning in their mental noise. You might feel like you're going crazy, or that you're just overly sensitive. But it's not that. It's your system trying to cope with an entirely new level of sensory input. You're picking up on things that most people filter out unconsciously. You're experiencing the world with a wider, more open perceptual field, and it takes a huge amount of energy to process that. You'll need to learn how to manage this, how to create boundaries, and how to protect your own energetic space. It's a learning curve, and it won't happen overnight.

Distinguishing Your Thoughts from Theirs

This is a big one. When your mind is suddenly an open channel, it's incredibly hard to tell what's yours and what's someone else's. You might find yourself having thoughts that feel alien, or emotions that don't seem to belong to you. This is where discernment comes in. You need to become a detective of your own mind. Ask yourself: "Is this thought truly mine? Does it align with my beliefs, my values, my usual way of thinking? Or does it feel like it came from outside?" Same with emotions. "Have I felt this way before? Is there a reason for me to feel this right now, based on my own experience? Or does it feel like an echo of someone else's mood?"

It takes practice, honestly. A lot of it. And you won't always get it right. There will be times you'll absorb someone else's anxiety and think it's your own. You'll carry their sadness around for a day before realizing it wasn't yours to begin with. But the more you pay attention, the more you become an observer of your own internal world, the better you'll get at distinguishing. It's about developing a strong sense of self, a clear inner anchor, so you don't get swept away by the currents of other people's minds. Journaling can help with this, just writing down what you're feeling and thinking, and then going back and reflecting on its origin. Meditation, even just a few minutes of quiet awareness, can also help you reconnect with your own inner space.

Grounding and Protection: Your New Best Friends

Alright, so you're feeling everyone's vibes, picking up on their thoughts. What do you do about it? You can't just shut it off, not completely. But you can learn to manage it. The first thing, and I can't stress this enough, is grounding. You need to be firmly rooted in your own body, in the present moment, in the physical world. Kundalini tends to pull you upwards, out of your body, into these expanded states. But to deal with the influx of information, you need to pull yourself back down. You need to be here, now.

How do you ground? Simple things. Go for a walk in nature, feel your feet on the earth. Eat nourishing food. Take a warm bath. Do something with your hands - gardening, painting, cooking. Anything that brings your attention to your physical senses and your physical body. Imagine roots growing from your feet down into the earth. Visualize yourself as a sturdy tree, unshakeable even in a storm. This isn't just hippie talk; it's a real energetic practice that helps stabilize your system. When you're grounded, you're less likely to be blown about by external energies. You create a stronger container for yourself.

Then there's protection. This isn't about building walls; it's about setting boundaries. You can mentally create a protective shield around yourself. Imagine a bubble of light, or a mirror, or whatever image resonates with you, that surrounds your aura. This isn't meant to block everything out, but to filter it. To only allow in what serves you, and to deflect what doesn't. You can also set intentions before you go into social situations: "I intend to only receive information that is for my highest good, and to maintain my own energetic integrity." It might sound silly, but intention is powerful. It tells your subconscious and your energy field how to operate.

I found great comfort in reading about others who went through similar things. Books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) really helped me understand I wasn't alone. It's a collection of essays that describes various spiritual crises, including the psychic phenomena that come with them. You're not the first, and you won't be the last, to experience this. Knowing that helps normalize it, even if just a little bit.

Finding Your Tribe: The Importance of Support

This is probably the hardest part, honestly. Who do you talk to about this? Most people won't understand. They'll dismiss you, or worse, pathologize you. You need to find people who get it. People who have been through similar experiences, or who are at least open-minded enough to listen without judgment. This might mean seeking out spiritual communities, online forums, or even just one trusted friend who you know won't think you've lost your marbles. You need a safe space to share what you're going through, to vent, to ask questions, and to feel validated.

I can't tell you how many times I felt like I was going crazy until I found others who were experiencing similar things. It's like finding an oasis in a desert. Suddenly, you're not alone. You're not a freak. You're just part of a smaller, less understood group of people who are having a very intense spiritual experience. That sense of belonging, that validation, is incredibly healing. It helps you keep your sanity when everything else feels like it's falling apart.

Remember, you're going through a massive internal shift. Your old support systems might not be equipped to handle the new you. That's okay. It doesn't mean they're bad people; it just means you need different kinds of support now. Don't be afraid to seek it out. It's not a sign of weakness; it's a sign of strength and self-preservation. Look for groups that discuss Kundalini, spiritual awakening, or psychic phenomena. You'll find them. They're out there. I also found The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) to be really helpful in understanding the physical and psychic manifestations of this energy. It’s an older book, but still very relevant.

Living with Expanded Awareness: Integration, Not Eradication

You're not going to "get rid" of this telepathy. It's part of your expanded awareness now. The goal isn't to shut it down completely, but to learn how to live with it, how to manage it, and eventually, how to use it constructively. It's about integration. You're integrating this new aspect of yourself into your daily life. It's a skill, like any other, that needs to be refined. You'll learn when to open up and when to pull back. You'll learn to discern what information is useful and what's just noise. You'll learn to trust your intuition more deeply.

This process takes time. A lot of time. Don't expect to master it overnight. There will be days when you feel completely overwhelmed, when you wish you could go back to blissful ignorance. That's normal. But there will also be moments of incredible insight, of deep connection, of knowing things that help you or others in deep ways. That's the other side of the coin, the gift that comes with the challenge. You'll start to see the world, and people, with a clarity you never had before. You'll understand motivations, fears, and hopes in a way that most people can't. That's a powerful thing, and it can make you a more compassionate, understanding human being.

I remember feeling so lost, so alone, during my most intense periods. But looking back, I wouldn't trade it. It forced me to grow, to confront my own fears, and to really understand what consciousness is. It's not just about the scary bits; it's about what you become on the other side. You're being remade, and while that's terrifying, it's also an incredible opportunity. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) are two more excellent resources that discuss this process of integration and how to cope with the various phenomena that arise. They helped me understand that these experiences, including telepathy, are often part of a wider spiritual awakening, not just an isolated event.

So yeah, you're knowing things you shouldn't know. It's unnerving. It's scary. But you're not alone, and you're not going crazy. This is part of the journey. Breathe. Ground yourself. Seek support. And know that with time, you'll learn to move through this with grace, even with this newfound, sometimes startling, awareness. You've got this. I know you do.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
